name: Templar Fleet Disappearance
description: The Templar Fleet Disappearance theory posits that a significant portion of the Knights Templar's naval fleet vanished from La Rochelle, France, around the time of their persecution and dissolution in the early 14th century (1307-1314). Proponents suggest the fleet, laden with Templar wealth and possibly sacred artifacts (like the Holy Grail or Ark of the Covenant), sailed to unknown destinations, escaping the clutches of King Philip IV of France and the Pope. Some theories extend this to suggest these ships reached the Americas, Australia, or other remote locations, influencing local cultures or establishing secret Templar colonies. The theory originates from speculation surrounding the Templars' known maritime activities and the sudden disappearance of considerable Templar assets following their arrest. Evidence cited includes anecdotal accounts, unsubstantiated claims of Templar symbols in foreign lands, and interpretations of nautical charts. Mainstream historians generally dismiss the theory. They attribute the missing ships and assets to confiscation by the French Crown, dispersal among other religious orders, or simply loss at sea. They highlight the lack of concrete historical evidence supporting a mass exodus or successful establishment of overseas colonies. The theory's appeal lies in its romantic notions of Templar resilience, hidden treasures, and the possibility of a secret history influencing global events.
subtitle: Vanishing ships, hidden treasures, secret escapes.

claims.claim: A large Templar fleet vanished from La Rochelle.
claims.evidence: Accounts mentioning Templar ships and the sudden disappearance of assets.
claims.criticism: Most ships were likely seized by the French crown or dispersed. No definitive records exist of a large-scale escape.

claims.claim: The fleet carried vast Templar wealth and sacred relics.
claims.evidence: Templars were known to be wealthy; stories of hidden treasures associated with the order.
claims.criticism: No solid evidence exists proving the Templars possessed any specific sacred relics, and most wealth was documented or seized.

claims.claim: The fleet sailed to the Americas or other distant lands.
claims.evidence: Alleged Templar symbols found in the Americas; speculative interpretations of old maps.
claims.criticism: These symbols are often misidentified or easily explained through other means. There is no archaeological evidence of Templar settlements in the Americas from this period.

keyFigures.name: Christopher Knight and Robert Lomas
keyFigures.role: Popularized Templar theories in their books.

keyFigures.name: Malcolm Barber
keyFigures.role: Historian specializing in the Knights Templar, offering critical analysis of Templar myths.

timeline.event: Arrest of Templars in France by Philip IV.
Official dissolution of the Knights Templar by Pope Clement V.
Emergence of legends and theories surrounding Templar survival and hidden treasures.

sources.author: Barber, Malcolm
sources.work: The New Knighthood: A History of the Order of the Temple
sources.title: Barber, Malcolm

sources.author: Partner, Peter
sources.work: The Murdered Magicians: The Templars and Their Myth
sources.title: Partner, Peter
